name: Ralph Cooper
description: American tap dancer, choreographer, actor, bandleader, singer, and writer (1908-1992)
abstract: Ralph Cooper (January 16, 1908 – August 4, 1992) was an American actor, screenwriter, dancer and choreographer. Cooper is best known as the original master of ceremonies and founder of amateur night at the Apollo Theater in Harlem, New York City, in 1935.

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Salute to Fats Waller

about_quotes.speaker: Hinton Milt
about_quotes.evidence: I worked in Ralph Cooper‟s ban in the Regal Theater. ... We played a little overture... yours truly
about_quotes.doc_title: Hinton Milt — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Don Albert
about_quotes.evidence: We played at the Follies Theater over there in Brooklyn with Ralph Cooper and his review.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: Ralph Cooper, who was a dancer turned orchestra leader, was playing at the Lafayette one week and he had a very good show band. Ralph really knew show business. I saw the band during the early part of the week and really enjoyed its presentation.

about_quotes.speaker: Nellie Lutcher
about_quotes.evidence: Ralph Cooper, I remember one time was producing the black show at the Burbank Theater. Ralph Cooper was also from New York... But he was very popular and a very -- they say he was a very marvelous producer

about_quotes.speaker: Fred Minor
about_quotes.evidence: The mention of Ralph Cooper occurs entirely within the [INTERVIEWER]'s turn ('Yeah, Pigmeat and Ralph Cooper. Yeah, Ralph Cooper, yeah.') with no response or acknowledgment from the interviewee.

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] I'll never forget -- do you remember when Ralph Cooper was the Master of Ceremonies... [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah.
